mnemonics.title: Hazardous E-Waste Elements
mnemonics.content: LEAD (Pb), MERCURY (Hg), CADMIUM (Cd), CHROMIUM (Cr) - Remember as 'Lead Makes Children Cry' to recall toxic heavy metals.

mnemonics.title: E-Waste Recycling Steps
mnemonics.content: COLLECT DISMANTLE SEPARATE RECOVER REFINES - Remember as 'Clever Dons Should Steal Riches Everywhere' to recall the sequential process.

mnemonics.title: EPR Meaning
mnemonics.content: EPR = Producers Take Financial/Operational Responsibility for End-of-life products - Remember 'Extended Producers Remember their products'.

acronyms.usage: Policy making producers responsible for collecting and recycling their products at end-of-life
Entity that helps producers meet their EPR obligations by coordinating collection and recycling
Old TV/computer monitor display technology containing leaded glass
Point when an electronic device can no longer be used productively
Most effective techniques for environmental protection in recycling operations
European Union directive for e-waste management, also used internationally

visualTips.visualization: Imagine magnets picking up iron like a magnet pulling iron filings, while eddy current is like using a magnet to push aluminum cans away because of induced currents creating repulsion.
Think of a pie chart: ~40% ferrous metals (iron/steel), ~25% plastics, ~20% non-ferrous metals (copper, aluminum), ~15% other materials including hazardous components.
Picture a body diagram: Brain effects (lead, mercury), Kidney effects (cadmium, mercury), Bone effects (cadmium), Lung effects (arsenic, beryllium) - toxins target specific organs.

analogies.analogy: Just like traditional mining extracts metals from the earth, urban mining extracts precious and valuable metals from our discarded electronics - treating e-waste as 'urban ore deposits'.
Similar to how bottle deposits work - you pay a small amount that's refunded when you return the bottle, EPR makes producers 'own' their products forever, responsible for final disposal.
E-waste grows at 3-5% annually while GDP grows at 2-3%, meaning our electronic consumption is outpacing economic growth - like a runaway train of obsolescence.
